danger_signals.visual_form: Small robots with white blockheads, red bodies, and yellow appendages
danger_signals.meaning: Minions of antagonist faction; immediate hazard to avoid or defeat

danger_signals.visual_form: Character faces transition from smiling to tired, unhappy, or weak expressions on HUD
danger_signals.meaning: Health degradation; urgent need to seek recovery items
Time-limited challenge; failing to reach goal within time limit results in failure

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger antagonist characters at level endpoints
danger_signals.meaning: Significant threat requiring defeat to progress; higher challenge intensity

safety_signals.visual_form: Ice cream, sweets, and food items scattered in levels
safety_signals.meaning: Safe to collect; restores character health and extends survivability
Permanent safety upgrade; increases total available lives for error recovery

safety_signals.visual_form: Each Warner Sibling character model distinct and color-coded (Yakko, Wakko, Dot)
safety_signals.meaning: Different abilities available; strategic tool selection for level progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Crates, switches, fuses visually distinct from hazards
safety_signals.meaning: Environmental elements that enable progression rather than cause harm

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Warner Bros. studio iconography and memorabilia collection
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Movie sets and studio lot locations; film parody themed stages

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Small robots with white blockheads, red bodies, yellow appendages
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple minion robots as visual representation of faction presence
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fifth and final levels where faction confrontation occurs; world domination plot context

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Movie memorabilia scattered across four selectable levels (Genesis) or script pages hidden in studio areas (SNES)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player chooses level order; collect-focused gameplay with optional objectives

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased density of enemy robots; boss encounters at level endpoints
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat intensity increases; must defeat bosses to obtain progression items

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fifth level unlocks (Genesis); final editing room stage unlocks (SNES); final boss arena distinct from standard levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss rush sequence (SNES) or direct Pinky and Brain confrontation (Genesis); world domination plot payoff

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character faces on HUD show damage progression; defeated character removal from active play
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rescue stages activate when characters defeated/captured; gameplay complexity increases with fewer available characters

environmental_storytelling.example: Different studio sets parody film genres (Space Opera, Action Movie, etc.); visuals communicate stage context and theme

environmental_storytelling.visual_example: Movie memorabilia (Genesis) or script pages (SNES) as progression markers; visual evidence of theft plot and world domination counter-plot
Yakko holds paddleball, Wakko holds mallet, Dot distinct character model; abilities visually telegraphed through equipment appearance
Warner Bros. studio setting establishes world context; movie sets as distinct visual zones communicate narrative framing

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Smiling facial expression on HUD; normal movement and action speed; capable of using special abilities (paddle, mallet, kiss)
Tired, unhappy, or weak facial expressions on HUD; should seek food items to restore health; reduced effectiveness indicated visually
Removed from active play; must be rescued via dedicated rescue stage; gameplay continues with remaining characters
Present in levels as ongoing hazard; visual presence signals danger area; controlled by Pinky and Brain faction
Larger visual presentation; require specific tactical approach (object-based damage); defeat triggers progression gate unlock
Capture mechanic hazard; defeats characters through capture rather than direct damage; triggers rescue stage activation

notes: Genesis version emphasizes collectible memorabilia fetch quest with non-linear level selection and Pinky/Brain theft counter-plot. SNES version emphasizes script page collection with linear progression and character stacking mechanic. Both versions use cartoon character models and colorful game worlds consistent with TV show aesthetic. Health system (Genesis) vs. capture system (SNES) represents different mechanical approaches to danger communication. Character-specific abilities (paddleball, mallet, kiss) create visual differentiation and strategic depth. Limited voice acting and music noted in contemporary reviews; visual communication carries more narrative weight. World state transitions tied to collectible progress gates (5-level unlock in Genesis, final stage unlock in SNES).
